coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial as stationary phase for open tubular capillary electrochromatography
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Yuanyuan
Li, Zhentao
Hu, Changjun
Li, Qiaoyan
Chen, Zilin
coredata.dc:description: The transformation of zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) to cross-linked nanomaterials is rare. In this work, a novel carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial (CON CDs-TAPB) consisted of 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)-benzene (TAPB) and carbon dots (CDs) through facile Schiff-base reaction was synthesized and then employed as a stationary phase for open-tubular capillary electrochromatography (OT-CEC). The CON CDs-TAPB and the CDs-TAPB coated column were characterized through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV-spectra experiments and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Thanks to CON CDs-TAPB unique structure and abundant accessibility and interaction sites, the prepared column exhibited a satisfactory separation ability towards analytes including parabens, phenolic compounds. Among all analytes, the highest column efficiency was over 1.6 × 105 plates·m−1. In addition, affording methylbenzene loading capacity of 156.9 pmole, surpassing most of those materials-based OT-CEC reported thus far. Thus, the prepared carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial (CON CDs-TAPB) gave a potential as a stationary phase in the separation science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Capillary electrochromatography
Covalent organic nanomaterials
Stationary phase
Carbon dots
Open-tubular column

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of solution and running buffer
Preparation of CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary and CDs coated capillary
Comparison with other materials-based OT-CEC column

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 82073808 and 81872828 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic procedure for the synthesis of the CON CDs-TAPB.
Schematic procedure for the preparation of the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary.
SEM images of the CON CDs-TAPB (a, 10,000×); the inner wall of bare capillary (b, 5000×) and the inner wall of CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary (c, 10,000×).
FT-IR spectra of the CDs (a), TAPB (b) and CON CDs-TAPB (c).

(a) Separation behavior of alkylbenzenes on the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary. Experimental conditions: mobile phase, 25% ACN in pH 3.0 10 mM phosphate buffer; applied voltage, −16 kV; injection, 27 mbar × 5 s; detection wavelength, 210 nm. Peaks: 1, methylbenzene; 2, ethylbenzene; 3, n-propylbenzene; 4, n-butylbenzene. (b) Separation behavior of chlorobenzenes on the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary. Experimental conditions: mobile phase, 30% ACN in pH 3.0 10 mM phosphate buffer; applied voltage, −16 kV; injection, 23 mbar × 5 s; detection wavelength, 210 nm. Peaks: 1, chlorobenzene; 2, 1,2-dichlorobenzene; 3, 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene.

(a) Separation behavior of phenols on the CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary. Experimental conditions: mobile phase, 35% ACN in pH 3.0 10 mM phosphate buffer; applied voltage, −17 kV; injection, 15 mbar × 5 s; detection wavelength, 214 nm. Peaks: 1, phenol; 2, 2,4-dimethylphenol; 3, 2,4-dichlorophenol. (b) Separation behavior of parabens on the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary. Experimental conditions: mobile phase, 33% ACN in pH 3.0 10 mM phosphate buffer; applied voltage, − 15 kV; injection, 7 mbar × 5 s; detection wavelength, 214 nm. Peaks: 1, methylparaben; 2, ethylparaben; 3, propylparaben.

The reproducibility and stability of the CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary towards alkylbenzenes. Experimental conditions: mobile phase, 25% ACN in pH 3.0 10 mM phosphate buffer; applied voltage, − 17 kV; injection, 17 mbar × 5 s; detection wavelength, 210 nm. Peaks: 1, methylbenzene; 2, ethylbenzene; 3, n-propylbenzene; 4, n-butylbenzene.
Reproducibility and stability of the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary column.
The linear equation, correlation coefficient (R2), limit of detection and limit of quantitation of the parabens analytes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Y: peak area; X: concentration of analytes (µg/mL).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: An overview of the reported methods for materials-based OT-CEC.

row.entry: The theoretical plate number range of analytes (N, plates·m–1)
Sulfa drugs and structurally related peptides
Neutral compounds, phenols, antiepileptic drug, triazine herbicides, active ingredients of Chinese medicine
Alkylbenzenes, chlorobenzenes, phenols, anilines, parabens, biomolecules
methylbenzene, naphthalene,4-methylbiphenyl, 3,5-dimethylbenzoic acid, 4-aminobenzoic acid, benzoicacid, 2-phenylethylamine, N,N-dimethylbenzenamine, phenylamine.
BPC, BPB,BPA, BPE and BPF; BPZ, BPAP, BPP and BPFL

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial as stationary phase for open tubular capillary electrochromatography

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedics Trauma and Microsurgery, Zhongnan Hospital of Wuhan University, School ofPharmaceutical Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Orthopedics Trauma and Microsurgery

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Orthopedics Trauma and Microsurgery, Zhongnan Hospital of Wuhan University, Wuhan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Combinatorial Biosynthesis and Drug Discovery, Ministry of Education, Hubei Province Engineering and Technology Research Center for Fluorinated Pharmaceuticals, Wuhan, 430071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Combinatorial Biosynthesis and Drug Discovery, Ministry of Education, Hubei Province Engineering and Technology Research Center for Fluorinated Pharmaceuticals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cKey Laboratory of Combinatorial Biosynthesis and Drug Discovery, Ministry of Education, Hubei Province Engineering and Technology Research Center for Fluorinated Pharmaceuticals, Wuhan, 430071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The transformation of zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) to cross-linked nanomaterials is rare. In this work, a novel carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial (CON CDs-TAPB) consisted of 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)-benzene (TAPB) and carbon dots (CDs) through facile Schiff-base reaction was synthesized and then employed as a stationary phase for open-tubular capillary electrochromatography (OT-CEC). The CON CDs-TAPB and the CDs-TAPB coated column were characterized through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV-spectra experiments and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Thanks to CON CDs-TAPB unique structure and abundant accessibility and interaction sites, the prepared column exhibited a satisfactory separation ability towards analytes including parabens, phenolic compounds. Among all analytes, the highest column efficiency was over 1.6 × 105 plates·m−1. In addition, affording methylbenzene loading capacity of 156.9 pmole, surpassing most of those materials-based OT-CEC reported thus far. Thus, the prepared carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial (CON CDs-TAPB) gave a potential as a stationary phase in the separation science.

ce:section.ce:para: Cross-linked covalent organic nanomaterials (CONs) have gained growing interest because of their unique properties and structures. Since the appearance of graphene [1], various advanced covalent organic nanomaterials (CONs) materials such as graphdiyne [2], microporous organic networks (MONs) [3,4] and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [5,6] have been explored. The large surface area, enhanced electron delocalization, plenty of active sites and high accessibility gave CONs attractive for separation [7], sensing [8], adsorption [9], energy storage [10] and photocatalysis [11].

Carbon dots (CDs) were admittedly considered as a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials that have drawn much attention in diverse research fields, such as bioimaging [12, 13], drug delivery [14,15], cancer therapy [16,17], catalysis [18,19] and separation science [20,21], since it was first reported in 2004 [22]. It should be noted that the application of CDs in the separation science field has gradually attracted attention recently, due to their desired characteristics including simple and facile preparation process, higher surface-to-volume proportion, better chemical and thermal stability, nanoscale size and abundant surface functional groups. Up to now, CDs-based materials have been utilized in solid-phase extraction (SPE) [23], dispersive solid-phase extraction (DSPE) [24], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [25] and gas chromatography (GC) [26]. However, cross-linked CDs-based CONs, let alone morphology control of CDs-based CONs, have not yet been reported [27].

With the rapid development of pharmaceutical, food and biotechnology industries, efficient separation methods and technologies have inevitably arisen. Open-tubular capillary electrochromatography (OT-CEC) with the properties of high efficiency and selectivity, ease of preparation, good permeability, low solvent consumption and tiny sample requirement, has emerged in chromatography separation science recently. However, the existing limitations of lower phase ratio and insufficient loading capacity are becoming the “bottlenecks” problem hindering the development of OT-CEC technique.

Herein, we reported a facile solvothermal method to synthesize novel carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial CON CDs-TAPB. A zero-dimensional aldehyde bearing carbon dots (CDs) and 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)-benzene (TAPB) were selected as the basic building blocks to construct cross-linked CON CDs-TAPB. The transformation of zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) to cross-linked nanomaterials significantly endows them with more accessibility and interaction sites. In addition, the introduction of the propeller model of TAPB can efficiently modulate morphology and give abundant π-π interaction sites. The newly developed CON CDs-TAPB was successfully employed as stationary phase for OT-CEC separation analysis. The higher surface area, abundant exposed binding sites of CON CDs-TAPB achieved rapid and highly efficient separation performance towards part of tested compounds, among which the maximum theoretical plate number towards methylbenzene reached 1.6 × 105 plates·m−1. Our work provides a new path to design and prepare cross-linked CDs-based CON, as well as accelerates the development of CDs in separation domain.

A group of sulfonamides analytes including sulfamethazine, sulfamethoxazole, 4-aminobenzenesulfonamide were acquired commercially from Aladdin. Four neutral alkylbenzenes (methylbenzene, ethylbenzene, n-propylbenzene and n-butybenzene), three chlorobenzenes (1,2,4-trichlorobenzene, 1,2-dichlorobenzene, chlorobenzene) and three phenols analytes including 2,4-dichlorophenol, 2,4-dimethylphenol, and phenol were all purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China, http://www.aladdin-e.com/). And three parabens (methylparaben, ethylbenzene and propylbenzene) were also commercially purchased from the same company. Besides analyte chemicals, other reagents like ethyl alcohol, thiourea, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), 1,4-dioxane, acetic acid, glutaraldehyde solution (25% v/v in H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) (12 mol/L) were purchased from the Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China, http://www.reagent.com.cn/). HPLC grade of acetonitrile and methanol were purchased from Tedia (OH, USA, http://www.tedia.com.cn/). Bare fused-silica capillary columns with a dimension of 50 µm i.d. × 365 µm o.d. were purchased from Ruifeng Chromatographic Devices (Yongnian, Hebei, China, http://liqingmi.b2b.chemm.cn/). Ultrapure water was purified with a Milli-Q ultrapure water system (MA, USA, http://www.emdmillipore.com/US/en/).

OT-CEC experiment was performed on Agilent 7100 CE instrument (Waldbronn, Germany, http://www.agilent.com/) coupling with diode array detector whose wavelength range was from 190 to 400 nm, and Agilent 7100 CE instrument was with chemical workstation software in charge of data collection and analysis) and temperature-controlled column which keeps analytical temperature stable. Characterization analysis including FT-IR, XPS, and SEM were implemented on Thermo NICOLET 5700 FT-IR Spectrometer (MA, USA, http://www.thermonexus.com/), Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) and Carl Zeiss Ultra Plus Field Emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Carl Zeiss, Germany, http://www.zeiss.com/), respectively. In addition, Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum was determined on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Besides, a mechanical pump obtained from Company (Longer Pump Company, Baoding, China, https://www.longerpump.com/) was utilized to pump specific solutions into fused silica capillary column. Commercially purchased Milli-Q ultrapure water system MA, USA, http://www.emdmillipore.com/US/en/) was employed to purify ultrapure water. A Mettler-Toledo pH meter (Shanghai, China, https://www.mt.com/cn/zh/home.html) was utilized to adjust buffer pH values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of solution and running buffer
ce:section.ce:para: The 4.0 mg/mL standard solutions were prepared by dissolving alkylbenzenes, chlorobenzenes, phenols and parabens in methanol, individually. Thiourea standard solution was as EOF marker and was prepared in methanol with a concentration of 3.0 mg/mL. The prepared standard solutions above were preserved in the 4 °C refrigerator. The phosphate buffers were prepared by dissolving Na2HPO4·12H2O in ultrapure water with different pH values modulated by adding phosphoric acid or sodium hydroxide, and then placed in the 4 °C refrigerator.

Prior to CEC experiment, a running buffer needed was prepared by adding a specific amount of acetonitrile into the prepared phosphate buffer with a certain concentration, and the sample solution was prepared by mixing standard analyte solutions with running buffer and then degassed by sonication.

The coated capillary was pumped with methyl alcohol for 1 min manually before experimenting. The modified column was flushed with methyl alcohol for 10 s every 5 cycles. During the experimental process, the hydrodynamic mode was facilitated to inject prepared sample solutions into capillary column and the separation run was implemented with an appropriate voltage at 20 °C. Between the intra-day use, the coated columns were flushed with methanol for 30 min on a mechanical pump, then dried with nitrogen stream and then preserved at room temperature.

The carbonated beverage was purchased from a local supermarket. The carbonated beverage was under ultrasonic treatment for 40 min to degas and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min to get supernatant. Then the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 µm membrane three times and preserved at 4 ℃ refrigerator before use.

The synthesis method of CDs was referred in the previous literature [28] with a few modifications. Glutaraldehyde (300 µL) and ethanol (600 µL) were added to a pressure vessel and heated in oil bath at 150 °C for 2 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resultant solution was evaporated at 65 °C, then the yellow semisolid was attained. The synthesis of CON CDs-TAPB is shown schematically in Fig. 1 . CDs (12 mg) and 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)-benzene (TAPB) (1 mg) were added into the 400 µL mixture of ethyl alcohol/1,4-dioxane (1 : 1 = v/v), with 20 s ultrasonic treatment, and light yellow clear solution was obtained. Then, 25 µL acetic acid (6 mol/L) was added into the above reaction solution, with 10 s shake vigorously. Then the reaction solution was kept in 60 °C water bath for 2 h and placed at room temperature for 3 days to obtain the final product. The light red solid was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. And then, washed with ethyl alcohol three times. To acquire dried product, the light red solid was placed in an oven at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary and CDs coated capillary
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process of CON CDs-TAPB modified column is graphically shown in Fig. 2 . Before the modification process, the uncoated capillary was pretreated by pumping 1 mol/L NaOH for 1 h and flushed with ultra-water for 30 min, 1 mol/L HCl for 1 h, ultra-water for 30 min, methanol for 30 min at 0.05 mL/h flow rate and dried by nitrogen for 1 h successively. Later on, the prepared capillary was filled with APTES aqueous solution with both ends sealed, reacting in water bath at 95 °C for 1 h (repeat once). The APTES coated capillary was then washed to remove redundant reagent by pumping ultra-water for 1 h and dried through a nitrogen stream. Then glutaraldehyde solution (2%, v/v in H2O) was pumped into APTES coated column at room temperature at 0.05 mL/h (repeat once), then the column was flushed by ultra-water for 1 h and blown with a continuous nitrogen stream. After that, the TAPB solution in dioxane (2 mg/mL) was flushed into the modified capillary with aldehyde groups with both ends blocked, then placed in oil bath for 1 h at 150 °C (repeat once). TAPB@APTES@capillary was prepared according to above procedure. Next, CDs (12 mg) and TAPB (1 mg) were added to the 400 µL mixture solution of 1,4-dioxane/ethyl alcohol (v/v = 1/1), then 25 µL acetic acid (6 M) was subsequently added into suspension above with vibrate for 10 s. Then, the reacting solution above was injected into TAPB coated capillary with amino groups and the capillary was put into water bath at 60 °C for 2 h and kept under room temperature for 3 days in a static condition. Then CDs-TAPB coated capillary was obtained. CDs coated capillary was acquired in the same way as CDs-TAPB modified capillary preparation without the addition of TAPB and acetic acid.

In this work, some parameters were involved, including column efficiency (theoretical plates, N) and retention factors (k), separate resolution (Rs), which were calculated by N = 16 × (
t W
)2, Rs =
2 (
t 2 − t 1
)
W 1 + W 2
, N = 16 × (
t W
)2, k =
(
tr − t 0
)
t 0
, individually, where w and t represent peak width and retention time of analytes respectively. The numbers 1 and 2 are on behalf of the first and second analytical compounds according to retention order individually. Besides, tr and t0 represent the retention time of the selected model analytes (methylbenzene, ethylbenzene, n-propylbenzene and n-butylbenene) and thiourea (EOF marker), individually. In addition, the EOF mobility was calculated according to the formula [29] µeof =
LeL
Vt 0
, where Le and L refer to the effective length and total length of coated capillary separately, and t0 represents the retention time of EOF marker (thiourea), and V represents applied voltage during CEC experiment.

The SEM was first conducted to confirm the morphology of the as-synthesized CON CDs-TAPB. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the SEM image of CON CDs-TAPB was spherical and the globular particles were with a diameter around 600 nm. The SEM images of bare column and CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary column are exhibited in Fig. 3b-c. Compared with the bare column (Fig. 3b), there were abundant nanoscale spherical particles uniformly and densely coated on the inner wall of the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary column (Fig. 3c). Besides, it can be seen from Fig. S1a that the CON coating layer was homogeneous along the column. And the SEM images in different CON modified capillary columns were all consistent (Fig. S1b). Besides, FT-IR was carried on to confirm the composition of CON CDs-TAPB. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the characteristic absorption peak in 1724 cm−1 of CDs attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O, which was corresponding to aldehyde groups on the surface of CDs, and PXRD experiment result of CDs (Fig. S4) was identical to previous report, which confirming successful synthesis of CDs. The FT-IR spectra of CON CDs-TAPB showed the appearance of a characteristic peak referring to C=N stretching at 1609.6 cm−1, revealing the formation of imine bonds (Fig. 4c). Moreover, the disappearance of NH stretching band (3210–3322 cm−1) of TAPB (Fig. 4b), as well as the significant attenuation of the C = O stretching band (1724 cm−1) of CDs further confirmed the completeness of Schiff-base reaction between two monomers. Besides, XPS experiment exhibited that CON CDs-TAPB was composed of C, N and O elements in Fig. S2, confirming the successful preparation of CON CDs-TAPB. The UV spectrum (Fig. S3) displayed that CDs had a stronger absorption at 218.5 nm, corresponding to the previous report [28]. In addition, another monomer TAPB had two strong absorption bands at 207.5 nm and 286.5 nm, which was attributed to π→π* transition of C=C and n→π* transition of amino groups on benzene ring respectively. CON CDs-TAPB had two absorption bands: one was at 222 nm which was due to the appearance of a larger conjugation system, and another at 374.5 nm was a broader absorption band which confirmed the formation of aggregates of CON CDs-TAPB. The above characterization results indicated that CON CDs-TAPB was successfully synthesized and the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary column was obtained.

It is an important factor that electroosmotic flow is the main impetus generated to promote mobile phase in CEC. In this work, the effects of buffer pH values on electroosmotic flow were investigated with pH values rising from 2 to 7. The EOF mobility was inspected in 10 mmol/L phosphate buffer with 25% organic modifier content. EOF marker (thiourea) and acetonitrile were selected as analyte and organic modifier, respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, the CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary was with plentiful amino groups on account of the existence of APTES and TAPB, thus was endowed with a medium anodic EOF. As the pH values of running buffer went up from 2 to 7, the EOF mobility decreased gradually, which was attributed to the decrease of the ionization of amino groups and the increase of dissociation of uncovered silanol groups on the surface of CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary. The EOF of CDs-TAPB@Capillary was less than that of TAPB@APTES@Capillary because amino groups was reacted and covered by polymer particles.

The retention factor is a general evaluation parameter of retention behavior. Thiourea was admittedly considered as EOF marker (t0). Four alkylbenzenes (methylbenzene; ethylbenzene; n-propylbenzene and n-butylbenzene) were selected as model anaytes to investigate retention behavior of CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary column. As is shown in Fig. S6. Four alkylbenzenes were eluted according to the order of log P value that is utilized to reflect the hydrophobicity of substances from low to high. On the other hand, with the growth of acetonitrile content from 20% to 40%, the retention factors for four alkylbenzenes decreased. This was because the growth of acetonitrile content contributed to a stronger elution ability, which exhibited an obvious reversed-phase retention behavior.

The loading capacity whose definition is the maximum sample injection amount when the half-height peak width (W1/2) of the relevant analyte grew by 10% over the W1/2 at low sample concentration [30], is a vital factor of open-tubular capillary evaluation. This experiment was performed by injecting methylbenzene with different concentrations, ranging from 0.3 mg/mL to 0.9 mg/mL. The W1/2 of methylbenzene with the concentration of 0.75 mg/mL, was lifted by 9.7% compared with the W1/2 of methylbenzene at 0.3 mg/mL, which is corresponding to the definition of loading capacity. The result is shown in Fig. S7. Thus, the maximum injection concentration of methylbenzene in this condition was 0.75 mg/mL. In addition, the loading capacity of CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary was 156.9 pmole.

In this work, four neutral alkylbenzenes (methylbenzene; ethylbenzene; n-propylbenzene and n-butylbenene) were selected to evaluate the separation performance of CON CDs-TAPB modified column in Fig. 5 , which the highest theoretical plates towards methylbenzene was over 1.6 × 105 plates·m−1, and baseline separation was successfully achieved towards four alkylbenzenes. The column modified with CDs cannot achieve baseline separation toward alkylbenzenes (in Fig. S9a) under the same condition. Besides, another group of neutral analytes including chlorobenzene, 1,2-dichlorobenzene and 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene was facilitated to test the separation performance of the CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary column. As shown in Fig. 5, three chlorobenzenes were with good resolution and high theoretical plates (up to 1.4 × 105 plates·m−1) while CDs coated capillary cannot achieve this three chlorobenzenes separation successfully (in Fig. S9b). It should be noted that these two groups of neutral analytes were successfully eluted based on the order of log P values increase, which demonstrated an obvious reversed separation mechanism and the highest column efficiency of chlorobenzene was 1.4 × 105 plates·m−1 on account of the strong π-π interaction and hydrophobic interaction provided by more benzene rings that were connected through Schiff-base reaction between amino groups of benzene ring and aldehyde group on the surface of carbon dots. Additionally, three types of phenols (phenol, 2,4-dimethylphenol and 2,4-dichlorophenol) were obviously baseline separated in Fig. 6 , however, CDs coated column displayed no separation tendency toward these three phenols under the same condition (in Fig. S9c), and the elution order was precisely in accordance with log P values from phenol 1.54, 2,4-dimethylphenol 2.49, and 2,4-dichlorophenol 3.10, individually, which were identical with hydrophobic separation mechanism. Moreover, a group of parabens including methylparaben, ethylparaben and propylparaben was successfully separated with the highest column efficiency (theoretical plates, N) towards methylparaben was over 1.1 × 105 plates·m−1 (Fig. 6) but CDs modified column cannot separate these three parabens under the same condition (in Fig. S9d). And three analytes were eluted according to the order of logP values growth since three parabens were not charged under this separation condition, which further indicated a hydrophobic separation mechanism. In addition, three kinds of sulfonamides including 4-aminobenzenesulfonamide, sulfamethazine and sulfamethoxazole were tested (Fig. S8), and the elution order was identical with the ascending sequence of logP values, which demonstrated a separation mechanism relied on hydrophobic interaction. Compared to CDs-TAPB coated column, CDs modified column cannot achieve baseline separation toward three sulfonamides (in Fig. S9e) under the same condition. All these groups of analytes were successfully separated and mostly relied on hydrophobic interaction and π-π interaction offered by CON CDs-TAPB coating layers on the inner wall of the capillary.

Reproducibility and stability are important evaluation parameters of OT-CEC which are evaluated by the relative standard deviation values (RSDs) of retention time. Here, the reproducibility and stability of the capillary modified by CON CDs-TAPB were inspected. The RSDs of the retention time towards four alkylbenzenes involving methylbenzene, ethylbenzene, n-propylbenzene and n-butylbenzene were employed to estimate the reproducibility and stability of the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary column. As exhibited in Table 1 , the RSDs of the retention time for four alkylbenzenes of intra-day (n = 3,%) and inter-day (n = 3,%) were both less than 6.95, and RSD values of retention time for four alkylbenzenes of columns within the same batch were ranging from 1.88 to 6.21. In addition, as demonstrated in Fig. 7 , the separation performance of coated capillary was almost the same without a few changes after 60 runs in CEC experiment. Therefore the above results all indicated the CON CDs-TAPB coated capillary was reproducible and stable.

In order to investigate the application potential of CDs-TAPB coated capillary, the prepared column was utilized to analyze parabens in real sample. Parabens are commonly used food additives existing in food and beverages. Thus, carbonated beverage was chosen as a real sample. The sample pretreatment procedure is stated in Section 2.5. The electropherograms of the parabens analytes in carbonated beverage are shown in Fig. S10.

Firstly, the linearity of this method was investigated. A series of different concentrations of parabens were injected, separated and detected. The linear equation, linear ranges, R2, LOD (limit of detection) and LOQ (limit of quantitation) of methylparaben, ethylparaben and propylparaben are demonstrated in Table 2 . The linearity ranges of these three parabens were all 2.5–100 µg/ml. LODs and LOQs were 0.21–0.27 µg/ml and 0.70–0.90 µg/ml, respectively.

The standard spiked experiments were then performed. The recoveries are listed in Table S1. The recoveries of these three parabens were ranging from 96.0%−107.7%, which indicated this analysis method showed great potential for real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other materials-based OT-CEC column
ce:section.ce:para: Until now, various materials have been developed and employed in OT-CEC separation. The prepared CON CDs-TAPB in our work was compared with other previous materials for OT-CEC [7,30-35] so as to verify the superiority of this work. As exhibited in Table 3 , obviously, CON CDs-TAPB modified column developed by our research group was novel and exhibited good separation performance towards phenols and parabens. Moreover, the preparation process of this material and OT column was quite facile and simple. The above features confirmed that CON CDs-TAPB can serve as a promising candidate for the chromatography separation domain.

In summary, a fresh variety of carbon dots-based covalent organic nanomaterial CON CDs-TAPB was successfully synthesized and explored as stationary phase for OT-CEC first. The characterization experiments confirmed the successful synthesis and modification of CON CDs-TAPB on the inner surface of the capillary column. Thanks to the plentiful accessibility and interaction binding sites provided by CON CDs-TAPB coating layers on the inner surface of capillary, enabled the prepared OT column to separate diverse organic compounds successfully with the properties of high resolution and more sufficient loading capacity. Additionally, the CON CDs-TAPB modified capillary exhibited satisfactory reproducibility and stability without obvious change in chromatography performance within 60 runs. Besides, CDs-TAPB coated column was utilized in real sample analysis, which gave a promise of this novel carbon dots-based covalent nanomaterial for the application in the separation field.

Yuanyuan Fu: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Zhentao Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Changjun Hu: Validation. Qiaoyan Li: Validation. Zilin Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 82073808 and 81872828).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2022.463343.
